sources: competitor research: goose-deep.json, opencode-deep.json, cline-continue.json, ava-self-audit.json

top_10_gaps.area: Per-turn file diff summary — missing entirely
top_10_gaps.current: Each write/edit tool call shows an individual DiffRow card. There is no aggregated 'N files modified' summary at the turn level. After a 10-file refactor the user sees 10 separate DiffRow cards with no overview and cannot quickly assess what changed across the turn.
top_10_gaps.ideal: OpenCode renders a collapsible 'N files modified' row at the bottom of each completed assistant turn. It has a 5-block proportional add/delete bar chart (e.g. ████░ +312/-48) and expands to show per-file before/after diffs. This is the most-clicked feature in OpenCode's UI according to their user research. Cline's DiffEditRow puts +N/-N counts in every file header. The pattern lets users review the whole turn at a glance before approving next steps.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Add a turn-level diff aggregator to MessageBubble. After agent status transitions to completed, collect all DiffRow data from that turn's tool calls, compute total +/- counts, and render a collapsible footer: 'Modified 4 files  ██████░░░░ +198/-23 ▾'. Expand shows the existing DiffRow cards reused inside the accordion. Rust bridge change required to emit turn-complete event with file list. Estimated: 3-4 days.

top_10_gaps.area: Tool card titles — raw names instead of semantic descriptions
top_10_gaps.current: Screenshots show tool cards labeled 'read', 'glob', 'bash', 'grep' — the raw tool name string. ContextGroupHeader shows 'Gathering context... (3)' without type breakdown. The user must expand the card to understand what is being read/run. In the multi-turn screenshot the tool cards are visible but their headers do not convey what is happening.
top_10_gaps.ideal: Goose generates verb-phrase tool descriptions: 'reading src/main.rs', 'running cargo test --workspace', 'searching for TODO'. OpenCode's ToolStatusTitle animates 'Gathering context...' → 'Gathered context — 3 reads, 2 searches' with a shared-prefix animation so only the changing suffix moves. Cline shows 'Reading src/main.rs...' with TypewriterText animation for the active read. The result: users understand tool activity at a glance without expanding any card.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Implement a getToolDescription(toolName, args) function for AVA's 9 default tools: read→'reading {path}', write→'writing {path}', edit→'editing {path}', bash→'running {cmd}', glob→'searching {pattern}', grep→'searching for {query}', web_fetch→'fetching {url}', web_search→'searching web for {query}', git_read→'reading git {subcmd}'. Apply to ToolCallCard header and ContextGroupHeader. Change ContextGroupHeader label to '3 reads, 2 searches, 1 list' breakdown. Estimated: 1-2 days.

top_10_gaps.area: Thinking blocks — raw pre-wrap text, wrong duration display, broken Ctrl+O hint
top_10_gaps.current: Screenshots confirm: thinking content renders as raw whitespace-pre-wrap text at reduced opacity. Complex multi-step reasoning with headers and bullet points is completely unreadable. The self-audit confirms ThinkingRow shows duration '0.0s' for completed messages because duration() uses component mount time rather than thinking start/end timestamps from message metadata. The hint text 'Ctrl+O to see full thinking' refers to an unregistered shortcut.
top_10_gaps.ideal: Continue's ThinkingBlockPeek shows a pill-shaped chip: 'Thinking...' (with AnimatedEllipsis) during streaming → 'Thought for 2.3s (1,847 tokens)' when complete. Clicking expands via a smooth max-h transition. The chip is compact, visually distinct, and collapses by default so it doesn't dominate the layout. Claude.ai and Cursor both render thinking with full markdown formatting, making long reasoning chains with headers and bullets readable. Goose shows gradient fade-out overlays at scroll boundaries for the thinking pane.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Three sub-fixes: (1) Apply renderMarkdown() to ThinkingRow expanded content — one-line change but dramatically improves readability for complex reasoning. (2) Fix duration to use message-level thinking timestamps (pass thinkingStartedAt/thinkingEndedAt from Rust bridge) and format as 'Thought for 2.3s'. (3) Replace 'Ctrl+O to see full thinking' with 'Click to expand' or register Ctrl+O as a real shortcut. (4) Style the collapsed thinking trigger as a pill/chip shape matching Continue's pattern. Estimated: 1-2 days.

top_10_gaps.current: The self-audit identifies a visible flash when LiveStreamingBlock unmounts and the settled MessageBubble mounts in its place. The animate-message-in is only 150ms with translateY(8px) — producing a jarring position jump visible to users at the end of every agent response. Visible in the multi-turn screenshot as positional discontinuity between the active streaming area and historical messages.
top_10_gaps.ideal: OpenCode and Goose use cross-fade or FLIP animations: the streaming block stays mounted during the transition, then the settled bubble fades in from the same position. The result feels like the text simply 'settles' rather than being replaced. This is the second most complained-about polish issue in async chat UI.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Use a FLIP approach: before LiveStreamingBlock unmounts, capture its getBoundingClientRect(). Mount the MessageBubble at the same position (translate3d offset) and animate it to its natural position with a 200ms ease-out. Alternatively: keep both mounted simultaneously with opacity cross-fade, using SolidJS's <Transition> component. The streaming signals can be kept alive briefly using a 'settling' state flag. Estimated: 1 day.

top_10_gaps.area: Tool card expand/collapse — instant mount/unmount, no animation
top_10_gaps.current: ToolCallCard, DiffRow, and CommandOutputRow use SolidJS Show conditionally for their content — content appears and disappears instantly when the chevron is clicked. There is an animate-tool-card-in on mount but zero collapse animation. The multi-turn screenshot confirms all tool cards are either fully open or fully closed with no transition.
top_10_gaps.ideal: OpenCode uses spring-physics animated height (Motion library, visualDuration:0.35 bounce:0) so expand/collapse feels physical. It also uses a requestAnimationFrame defer before mounting heavy content (large diffs) to keep the open animation at 60fps. Goose collapses bash output to 4rem max-height during execution then expands to 20rem on completion with a max-height CSS transition. Cline's DiffEditRow has a 320px max-height with expand handle and animated scroll-follow during streaming.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Use the CSS grid trick for all collapsibles: wrap content in a div with grid-template-rows: 0fr→1fr and transition: grid-template-rows 200ms ease. The inner div has overflow:hidden and min-height:0. No JS height measurement needed — works with any content size. Apply to ToolCallCard, DiffRow, CommandOutputRow, ThinkingRow. Estimated: 1 day.

top_10_gaps.area: Tool status indicator — no per-icon status dot, shimmer only on thinking
top_10_gaps.current: Tool cards show a spinner icon while running but it occupies a full icon slot. There is no at-a-glance status visible in the collapsed card header. The thinking-shimmer CSS exists but is NOT applied to tool title text during tool execution — confirmed by the self-audit ('AVA has a CSS class thinking-shimmer applied to the ThinkingRow label but nothing on tool titles'). Tool cards in the screenshots show static text headers with no animation while running.
top_10_gaps.ideal: Goose composites a 2x2px status dot (green=success, red=error, yellow+animate-pulse=loading, gray=pending) on the tool icon. This gives fast-scan status at a glance across a list of 10+ tool cards without expanding any. OpenCode's TextShimmer applies a continuous CSS shimmer sweep on the tool title text while status=running, with a 220ms debounce on completion so it doesn't cut off abruptly.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Two changes: (1) Add a 2x2px absolute-positioned dot on the tool-call-icon.tsx component, colored by status (running=amber+pulse, done=green, error=red, pending=gray). (2) Apply the existing thinking-shimmer CSS class to ToolCallCard's header text span while isRunning() is true, removing it on completion. Both changes are CSS-only with minimal JSX. Estimated: 0.5 days.

top_10_gaps.area: Textarea auto-resize — abrupt height jump, no smooth transition
top_10_gaps.current: The input area in all three screenshots shows a single-line input that jumps in height when text is typed across the line boundary. The self-audit confirms: 'no smooth height transition — the textarea jumps in height on input.' The auto-resize logic in use-input-state.ts fires correctly but there is no CSS transition on the height property.
top_10_gaps.ideal: Claude.ai and ChatGPT use a shadow-div approach: an invisible div with identical styling measures the content height, and the visible textarea transitions smoothly to that height via transition: height 120ms ease. The textarea cap at 200px shows a visible scrollbar only above the cap. This is a high-frequency interaction — every user who types more than one sentence encounters it.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Add transition: height 100ms ease to the textarea element in InputTextArea. Replace the scrollHeight-based resize trigger with a shadow-div measurement to avoid the one-frame height jump on the first keypress. The shadow div is an identical hidden div that wraps the same text, measured via offsetHeight. Estimated: 0.5 days.

top_10_gaps.area: Approval UX — separate dock loses tool context, no allow-once vs always-allow distinction
top_10_gaps.current: ApprovalDock appears as a bottom bar between message list and input. The user sees the tool name and a yes/no choice but must scroll up to see WHAT the tool was going to do (the args). The permission level is a single checkbox 'Always allow' without distinguishing allow-once from persistent allow. Post-approval, no historical record of what was approved appears in the message stream.
top_10_gaps.ideal: Goose embeds approval inline inside the same tool card with an amber border (border-amber-500/50) — the user sees exactly what they are approving without scrolling. The approval buttons are 'Allow Once', 'Always Allow', 'Deny' as separate named buttons, not a checkbox+confirm pattern. After decision, the card shows static text ('Allowed once', 'Denied') so audit history is readable in scroll-back. Continue shows the pending tool call in the toolbar above the input (less disruptive than a full dock).
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Two-phase improvement: (1) Quick win — rename the ApprovalDock checkbox pattern to explicit 'Allow Once' / 'Always Allow' buttons with matching Rust backend PermissionLevel variants. Add post-decision text to the ToolCallCard header. (2) Medium effort — add inline approval mode to ToolCallCard: when a tool is pending approval, amber-border the card and show the approval buttons in the card footer rather than the dock. Keep the dock as fallback for tools that arrive without a card context. Estimated: 2 days.

top_10_gaps.area: Context compaction — no visual marker in message stream
top_10_gaps.current: The /compact command and auto-compaction both occur silently. There is no divider or visual indicator in the chat history showing where compaction happened. Users have no awareness that the model's context window no longer includes messages before the compaction point. The opencode-deep.json audit flags this as 'missing' with high importance.
top_10_gaps.ideal: Continue renders a 'Previous Conversation Compacted' horizontal rule at the compaction point AND dims all messages before it to 35% opacity (opacity-35). This is a strong dual signal: the divider marks the boundary, and the dimming communicates 'the model cannot see these'. OpenCode renders a 'History compacted' divider inline. Both approaches take 1-2 hours of implementation time but solve a genuine user confusion — users often re-ask questions the model has forgotten, not realizing compaction occurred.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: Emit a compaction_event from the Rust AgentStack when auto-compaction fires (it already logs this internally). Add a 'compaction' message type to the store. In ChatView, render a styled <hr> with label 'Context compacted — model sees from here' at the compaction boundary. Optionally dim messages before it using opacity-50 class. Estimated: 1 day (0.5 for Rust event, 0.5 for UI).

top_10_gaps.area: Sidebar session list — visually dense, no project grouping, title truncation
top_10_gaps.current: The sidebar visible in all three screenshots shows session entries as single-line items. The session titles truncate mid-word without ellipsis at the list width. There is no visual grouping by project or date ('Today', 'Yesterday', 'Last week'). The sidebar has reasonable spacing but lacks the affordances that make navigating 20+ sessions practical. No message count badge or last-activity timestamp is shown per entry.
top_10_gaps.ideal: OpenCode groups sessions by time period ('Today', 'This week', 'Older') with a subtle separator. Goose shows a session card with title on top, relative timestamp on bottom-right, and a hover tooltip for long titles. The Claude.ai sidebar shows title + truncated first message and groups by date. The pattern that matters most: date grouping and message count badges so users can quickly orient to 'where they left off'.
top_10_gaps.recommendation: In SidebarPanel's session list: (1) Add date group headers ('Today', 'Yesterday', 'This week', 'Older') using session.created_at timestamps — group computation is O(n) and can be a createMemo. (2) Add a message count badge (small pill: '14 msgs') to each session row. (3) Fix title truncation to use text-ellipsis with max-w properly set (current truncation is abrupt). (4) Show relative timestamp ('2h ago') per entry. All CSS/store work, no Rust changes needed. Estimated: 1 day.

things_ava_does_better: Interleaved thinking+tools rendering: AVA correctly reconstructs the think→tool→think→tool→response sequence live during streaming AND in historical replay. Neither Goose nor OpenCode handles this for historical messages — they only show thinking inline during streaming.

Three-tier mid-stream messaging (steer/follow-up/post-complete): architecturally unique. No competitor has post-complete grouped pipelines (G1, G2, G3). The Alt+Enter / Ctrl+Alt+Enter bindings expose this cleanly.

Streaming dual-path design: live content from agent signals, completed messages from store. This prevents the N² re-render problem that affects React-based competitors (Goose especially) where every token causes a full component tree re-render.

Design token system: primitives → semantic → component layers with 29 themes, dark/light/midnight/charcoal, per-accent presets, syntax token overrides, reduced-motion support, and high-contrast mode — all in a structured CSS architecture. Competitors typically have 2-3 hardcoded themes.

ThinkingRow auto-expand during streaming / auto-collapse on completion: matches user expectation (see progress, then get out of the way). Goose and OpenCode do the opposite — thinking starts collapsed and requires user action to see it stream.

ContextGroupHeader context-tool grouping is functionally correct: consecutive read/glob/grep calls batch into a single collapsible. The visual presentation needs polish (see gap #2) but the underlying grouping logic is equivalent to OpenCode's groupParts algorithm.

ErrorRow is contextually rich: distinguishes cancelled vs errored, shows live countdown for rate-limit retryAfter, surfaces actionable hints (/model, /compact, /connect) matched to the specific error. Competitors show generic error text.

ApprovalDock has proper keyboard shortcuts (Enter=approve, Escape=deny) and aria-dialog labelling. Risk-adaptive auto-expand for high/critical operations. No competitor fully implements keyboard-only approval flows.
content-visibility: auto on [data-message-id] elements gives free off-screen rendering suspension — a meaningful win for long sessions without the complexity of virtual list code.

Per-tool-type specialized renderers: ToolCallCard (generic), CommandOutputRow (terminal with live elapsed timer), DiffRow (add/delete stats + diff viewer), ContextGroupHeader (grouped reads) — more specialized coverage than Goose's single ToolCallWithResponse component.
grade_rationale: AVA's architecture is genuinely strong — the streaming pipeline, reactive design, design token system, and specialized tool renderers put it technically ahead of Goose and on par with OpenCode's depth. The grade is B rather than A because three visible rough edges accumulate into a 'not quite polished' feel for new users: (1) the streaming-to-settled flash is jarring at the end of every response, (2) tool cards show raw names rather than semantic descriptions making them harder to scan, and (3) thinking content is unreadable as raw text. These three issues together are visible in every screenshot and would be the first things noticed by a user coming from Claude.ai or Cursor. They are all fixable in under a week of focused work. The missing per-turn diff summary (gap #1) is the single largest structural gap vs OpenCode and is the feature users cite most when comparing AI coding tools.
